original_reasoning: خنطه denotes distressing or pressing, and εἴχοντο conveys being held; constraining pressure and being held match reasonably well.
βιάζεσθαι means 'to be forced' or 'to use force', which overlaps with the Arabic gloss 'to be set upon/overpowered', though not with 'broad, wide'.
Fixity and staying put align with holding or containing fish.
Arabic is greasy or rich with fat, Greek is honey-sweet; both converge on richness, lusciousness, and pleasant density.

الرقيب=watcher, guardian (حرسه). πολιορκέω=to besiege, blockade (sustained military watching/encirclement). Both involve sustained vigilant observation with control intent. Guarding and besieging are two faces of watch-and-control, from defender and attacker perspectives.
the gloss has vigorous striking/mixing, which plausibly extends to collision or clashing together.

الطنء includes 'لا يعيش صاحبها' (its possessor does not survive - i.e., fatal condition). τελευτή=end, death, completion of life. Direct semantic overlap: a state from which one does not survive = death/end. Strong match in the mortality domain.
A miserly person is conceptually harsh or pressing; oppress/crush is a plausible extension.
